quotes.verbatim_quote:
First play the pattern starting from every degree of the scale (in other words modally transposing
it). In the example below all of the notes are played over a dominant sus 4 chord. This allows you to

hear the sound of the key without having to deal with any avoid notes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Modal sequence application across scale degrees over sus4.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Next leave out some of the notes within the pattern.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Selective note omission as rhythmizing move.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Leaving out one note in two groups of four creates groups of seven.
Leave out some notes and add rests.
As you can see, this approach leads to a very expansive way of hearing sequences.
Ascend or descend within the sequence.
Skip up or down to any note in the scale to start the sequence.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Free entry point on any scale degree.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Try applying polyrhythmic approaches to the sequence.

When practicing these exercises start
otf with familiarizing yourself with the sequence and then work your way through the possibil-
ities taking as many liberties as you'd like. Try composing 12 modal sequences and then playing
each of them on one or more of the play along tracks.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Internalize first, then expand freedom; 12-sequence target.
